sentences.text: Oh, why not?
Well, he didn't help me, but he promised me that they would not bother me anymore because.
I was-- every day they told me to go down there, declare my [INAUDIBLE].
Even-- even my brothers were-- because we were born in Germany, and we spoke German.
[INAUDIBLE] any German.
He was the chief engineer, yes.
Before the war with Poland, there was a dedication of an airfield in-- I believe in-- in eastern Germany where Hitler was present.
And Hitler asked, who was the [NON-ENGLISH]?
Who was the chief engineer?
So they told him it was [NON-ENGLISH].
So he asked him to be at such-and-such a meeting in Berlin.
So then [NON-ENGLISH] went every six weeks to Berlin to a special meeting, probably a meeting about airfields and so on.
Now-- [COUGHS] excuse me.

When I was-- as I said, I became friends with my wife-- wife's sister and her family, it was about later on in 1943, I believe, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '43, when they started to transport the Jews to the death camp.
[COUGHS] Excuse me.
And at this time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew that something had to be done.
But before that, I was living on the street, and my wife's family moved to the same house.
The different one that we were-- I was renting one room from the main entrance.
And they were renting other rooms from-- from the outside of the house.
And one day, a Gestapo came to the house, and-- and they thought that Rozen lived there.
That was the name.
And I said, when did they live there?
So they went up there.
There were two guys.
One was a-- in a Gestapo uniform, and the other one was  in a civilian.
And they went up there, and I wanted to see what's going on.
So I got out, and I was listening under the window.
And then when my wife jumped out-- and I think my wife told you this story?
No, you go.
Please tell-- tell me your version.
So we went to a-- to a old Jewish cemetery.
So she jumped out of the window.
And you knew that she was--
I was there.
I grabbed her, and we we-- we run away from there, yes.
And we went to the Jewish cemetery.
And then when everything was calm, we can come back.
But she didn't sleep there anymore.
She slept someplace else.
And from then on, she was in hiding all the time.
And I went to Warsaw, and I got some papers for her, false papers.
She was Christina Zofia Paderewska.
Well, I-- had some-- some-- some contacts, some contacts with people that were in the underground.
Then I got the papers.
Actually nothing was-- was free, because those things cost money.
And then I got the papers.
Then later on, when they started to transport Jews to the Treblinka, mainly, I got papers for my-- for her mother and for her father.
And they all went to Warsaw.
But it's a long story.
I would have to talk for days.
After, I went with my wife to Warsaw, I went back to pick up her mother.
And it was not such an easy thing.
Because from Zamosc, we had to change trains, but I now went-- came to Warsaw.
And from Warsaw, after a few days, we went to a town, Czestochowa.
And I found a room for her in a village.
The village name is Olsztyn, near Czestochowa.
And there, she-- she was as my aunt.
She couldn't move around too much because she has a little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you could see that she was Jewish.
So she was in that one house, and I visited her.
And my wife was, at the same time, living in Czestochowa.
So we visited her mother on the weekends.
And it was going on for-- for, I don't know, maybe a year or so.
And you were still working at the airfield?
No, I had-- I had to leave the job up there in Zamosc.
I had to leave the job because everybody was talking, everybody knew.
They knew that I helped the Jews.
They even told my brother.
They were-- they were blackmailing my brother.
One fellow came and blackmailed my brother that he knows that I took Mrs.
Rozen out, and so on.
So I couldn't stay there anymore, no, I had to leave.
Well, I was doing odd jobs.
During the war, selling that, doing that.
At-- at one time, I got an idea that I-- I had  a camera.
It was the only thing that was left.
I had a camera.
And I used the camera as my tool.
I was making photographs.
But before that, we went with my wife to Warsaw, where she stayed with my-- with my friend, a woman that was my sister friend, and like a family friend.
And at this time, my wife-- my wife was married at this time.
I got a call from the husband that he's-- he's held by a agent, Gestapo agent.
And he wanted me to come and bring a diamond ring to bail him out.
So she gave me the diamond ring.
I went to bail him out.
It was a very-- a very scary situation, because he could have taken me.
But I went there, and I got him out.
Did you know him before?
Had you met him before?
Her husband?
Yes, I knew him, yeah.
I helped him.
Yeah, of course.
And-- but then was another episode.
A grand-- grandson of Peretz, the Jewish poet.
Do you know who he was?
His grandson came to me in the town Czestochowa, asked me for help.
So I went with him to Warsaw, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got him some-- I was preparing to get him some papers.
But I was arrested, and he was arrested.
And her husband was arrested.
Her husband was also in Warsaw with you?
Yes, yes.
So-- but there was a very bad situation, very bad.
But I got out of it.
Well, I was arrested in Warsaw, you know.
Anytime you're arrested, you know if you get out, 90%, you didn't get-- get out.
But I had a friend.
He was a general.
He was a-- at this time he was a colonel.
Later he became a general.
He was the chief of staff of the Polish underground and the Warsaw district.
And he managed to get me out.
I got out of there after, I believe, two weeks.
And so the-- but Peretz, Peretz was kind of a loudmouth, and he told everybody he was Jewish.
Everybody, I don't know, but he told that he was Jewish.
And they sent him to Majdanek, and very shortly after that, he was dead, literally.
But I have to go back a little bit now.

While I was still in Zamosc, working for the Bauleitung der Luffwaffe, we-- I got the idea-- I told my-- my boss that I would like to go to Warsaw, and maybe I can get some electricians in the Warsaw ghetto.
So he said, OK.
It was-- it was-- no, that was in 19-- '42, yes.
The world war with Russian broke in '41. '39, '41, yeah, '42.
On our way to--
No.
No, I was involved from the beginning.
But the moment that I went to Zamosc, I was involved with Jews all the time.
But I don't know.
Somehow it so happened that I-- my brother did not, but I did.
And I went to Warsaw with two German drivers, the driver and driver-- backup driver with a big truck.
And we had an accident.
They were going too fast.
It was early in the morning.
It was still a little bit dark, and we rolled over a few times.
But before I left, everybody knew, Damski's going to Warsaw.
Damski's going to be in the ghetto.
So I-- they all came to me.
They wanted to-- if I take them to the ghetto to see their family, you know.
And I said, no, I cannot take.
I say-- I take-- I had one, my favorite, his name was Mendel Faigelbaum.
The older one was Friedman.
They both lived in Israel, and two other guys.
But I told him not to take anything with him because I don't want to get caught, you know, with food smuggling to the Warsaw ghetto.
No, no, they won't do it.
But when we-- but I myself, I organized 70 kilograms of pork fat.
And during that accident, one-- no, two of them got hurt very badly.
Faigelbaum had a big scar like that over here.
And the other one got hit with the acid in a bottle in the back, couldn't move.
And my pork was in a ditch.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
At the same time, there was a column of German soldiers going to the east front.
And a colonel stopped in the passenger car and asked, what's going on?
So I told them what we had.
He thought that I was a German.
So they ordered the-- the soldiers to put the-- the truck on the wheels.
The truck couldn't move.
And he asked me if I have any-- any injured people.
I said, yes.
So he told the chauffeur to take these injured people to the hospital.
Now what am I going to do?
I cannot take them to the hospital, Polish side.
The only way-- thing I could do is take them to the ghetto.
So I went with them.
They still thought-- they didn't know that they are Jews.
But I directed the chauffeur where to go.
I told him to stop in the front of the-- of the gate to the ghetto and got out.
They did not-- didn't have any problem to get into the ghetto, Jews, because there were some going to work, you know.
But when I got out, there was an SS man that stood out there.
Look at me.
And he said, you brought those Jews in a German car?
And he went after me with this [NON-ENGLISH], but I ran away.
And there I was in the ghetto.
I was a-- was an athlete.
I could run.
In a few seconds, I was in the ghetto.
I was-- the first thing in the ghetto, I saw a fellow lying on the sidewalk with an old guy with the hand stretched out and said, give me a [GERMAN].
You understand?
But can you say it for the camera, though?
Give me a piece of bread.
It was an awful thing.
You couldn't believe that you suddenly come from a normal world to a  world that you thought did not exist.
If you imagine that there was 500,000 population in the Warsaw ghetto, in a very small territory, and crowded with limited food, electricity, and water.
You could see, and I saw myself, a pushcart with dead bodies they were collecting every morning.
I saw it myself.
So I spent-- I was in the ghetto.
Again, I-- don't know what to do, I didn't know what to do, where to go.
But I managed to find some soul that let me sleep in-- in a house.
And I knew the telephone number of one of the guys' girlfriends in the ghetto, so I called her up.
And she said they were both in the hospital in the ghetto, but they were OK.
And then I got in contact with those four fellows.
In the meantime, I had to have a-- an armband with the Star of David, because I was moving in the ghetto.
It's it's a-- it's an awful feeling.
You feel like-- like a marked man.
And it was-- makes a lifetime impression on you.
I think the Jews understand that.
The ones that went to the holocaust, not the ones  over here.
Oh, I got the band somewhere.
I don't remember.
You could just borrow from anybody because everybody had that band.
And in the meantime, I called up my superior and where I was working.
And he said-- told me that he's sending another truck.
In the meantime, those two German soldiers were in a armory in Warsaw.
I got in contact with them on the telephone, and two or three days later, I directed them to come to pick me up in the ghetto.
Would they care to pick me up in the ghetto?
First, we went out to recover my-- my pork.
I'm telling it because it has some connection with something that happened later.
And it wasn't there.
But with the help of a gun, I found it.
Not my gun, but those two guys, those two German soldiers.
Yeah.
They found it right away.
And we went-- and it was a very, how can I say, touchy situation.
I directed them.
I told them to wait for me in such-and-such a place.
Rather, they told me, the guys that were in the ghetto.
So we went to the-- to the ghetto and backed up with the truck like that, backed up with the truck.
And I sold that merchandise, the pork.
In the Warsaw ghetto.
In the ghetto, yes.
But all  the transactions took a minute.
Everything had to be ready, because split of a second was life and death.
And when everything was done, I said to that fellow, Faigelbaum, OK, everybody ready?
Let's go out.
And I noticed that Faigelbaum, he was pale, but this time he was pale like this.
And I said, what's wrong?
He didn't say anything.
And I looked under the canvas of that truck, you know, those trucks covered with canvas.
And there were Jews laying on the flat in the truck.
What are you going to do?
Tell them [NON-ENGLISH] like a German?
I told them, OK-- it was a split second decision-- keep quiet.
But I knew that-- that-- that Faigelbaum was-- was so afraid, so scared.
I told him, OK, just keep quiet.
He got into the truck.
And we were stopped by the Gestapo-- by the-- it wasn't Gestapo, SS rather, pardon me-- SS at the gate.
They looked at us.
Well, I looked like a civilian with two German soldiers.
And I said, we're just going through the ghetto.
We're not stopping anywhere.
Asked me for any papers, well, the two German soldiers had the papers.
And so we went out.
We went-- got-- got out of Warsaw to a town named [NON-ENGLISH]
They didn't know it.
They did not know what was going on at all.
But when we-- when we got out at [NON-ENGLISH], they were about 10, 12 miles from Warsaw.
I told them, let's stop the truck, and I told the guys, listen, fellows.
That's as far as I can risk.
They were out of the ghetto.
And I told them, I want everybody to contribute something so I can give it to the two German soldiers.
And it wasn't too much.
One fellow didn't have any money at all, maybe he had.
But he said, he doesn't have any.
And the other guy, everybody was happy to be giving me some money, and I gave the money to the two German soldiers.
This way they kept their mouth shut.
Then they knew what happened, but since I gave them the money, they wouldn't brag about it.
And that was it.
It was one of the most, I can say, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] thrilling adventures in my career during the war.
I don't know exactly, 10 or 12. 10 or 12, yeah.
And--
Oh, I know two of them went to Israel.
That's Friedman and Faigelbaum.
And they both, they went to Israel.
And what happened to the other ones, I don't  know.
So what else would you like to know?
Right.
This situation when I was in Warsaw.
And with Peretz, it was later.
Yes.
Peretz went to Majdanek.
But Herdenhut was the name of my wife's husband.
He got out with me.
And he went to a town, [NON-ENGLISH], where his mother was.
As a matter of fact, his mother was, for a while, in the house in Olsztyn, where my so-to-speak aunt was living.
It was a house where people that got out didn't know where to go.
It was the first stop where they start to get a little-- you know, a little fresh air and a little courage.
It was like a-- what do you call a-- there's a name for the house when they get released from a jail.
Halfway house, yeah.
That's what it was, yeah.
When we were living in that Olsztyn, I met quite a few people that were Jewish.
One of them was Frank Morgans, Frank Morgans, who is-- lives in-- in New York.
He wrote a book.
And he was one of the founders of Jewish Foundation for Christian Rescuers, together with Rabbi Schulweis.
And that town where, as I found out now lately, there were about 10 Jewish families, the town was no more than-- than-- than a thousand population.
But had-- had a mayor, and a city hall, and that family of-- of his name, [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH].
Everybody was on false papers.
And he had with him his wife, his mother, two daughters, and, I believe, his mother-in-law.
And they all survived.
And then I knew of other Jewish families in that little town, and they all survived.
Nobody denounced them.
As a matter of fact, there was the famous opera singer, Maria Modrakowfska who was later a wife of a Polish ambassador to Belgium.
They were hiding up in that little town, too.
And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke to about her about Jews.
And I got a letter from her son, who's a professor of university in Krakow.
He wrote to me about his father.
And his father was a-- rather a friend of Jews.
Because at the meeting of the Polish Underground, somebody said about what he did to the Jews, throwing somebody's table.
And he gave him a speech and told him that thanks to people like you, we have a bad name in the world.
So there were people that they all knew.
I found out from his son that his mother was speaking to the mayor.
They had-- one evening, they had something to drink, and they knew about every Jew in that town that we didn't know.
As a matter of fact, some people thought that I was Jewish.
Morgan-- Morgans thought that I was Jewish.
His wife thought that I was not Jewish.
My wife was Jewish, so anyhow.
So we were speaking of each other, but we never mentioned to each other.
Because you say something to someone and he get arrested, and you in, see?
So we never spoke to-- to-- we knew Jews that we never admitted that we are Jewish or we know that you are Jewish.
That was the way to-- to-- to survive, too.
What conditions were you living in in this town at the time?
What conditions were you living in at this time?
Well, I was making photographs for living.
Everybody had to have a [NON-ENGLISH], which means an ID card with this photograph.
So I was making photographs of the peasants around, and so I made a living out of it.
One day, one of the-- I was doing pictures at a wedding, and one of the fellows took me-- or the guest took me to the side and told me.
You know, he said that they're talking that your wife is Jewish.
Oh, that's the only way to get out.
No, she was not my wife at the time.
We married after the war.
Yeah.
Oh, we always were hiding, you were always in hiding.
Nobody knew.
My family knew.
My family.
I was not by myself.
Of course.
As a matter of fact, we lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
One day, there was a-- a-- the custodian of the house, or the caretaker, was waiting for me and he says that the Gestapo was looking for you.
So I went inside the house.
I put everything in a bed sheet and got out.
Well, he was standing in front to see if nobody was coming.
It-- it-- it was a situation that you-- every second counts sometimes.
So you-- you basically joined forces.
You both had to be hiding, so you were hiding together.
Of course, yeah.
Well, when-- I didn't finish that-- that her husband in the town of [NON-ENGLISH] was-- was-- was-- went under the-- he knew where his mother was and what cell she was arrested, sitting.
And he went under the window and spoke to her.
And a-- a Gestapo agent shot him.
But he managed to run away to a village and lay in a barn in that village.
And the peasant's wife came to our house, and told us that he was lying there with a bullet.
So we had to get a doctor and go to him.
But when we got there, he was dead.
It was, you know, another situation where you couldn't get a doctor.
Excuse me, a doctor could not aid any Jew.
